def: A structured collection of related data used for analysis or model training.
ex: The team split the {{dataset}} into training and testing subsets before modelling.

def: An individual measurable property or input variable used by a predictive model.
ex: Selecting the most informative {{feature}} improved the classifier's overall accuracy.

def: The target output or correct answer assigned to a training example.
ex: Each image in the {{label}}ed dataset carries a class {{label}} for training.

def: A mathematical representation learned from data to make predictions or decisions.
ex: The trained {{model}} predicted customer churn with strong reliability.

def: The process of fitting a model to data by adjusting its parameters.
ex: Model {{training}} took several hours on the GPU cluster.

def: Evaluating a trained model on unseen data to estimate real performance.
ex: During {{testing}}, the model was scored on data it had never seen.

def: Assessing model performance on held-out data to tune settings and prevent overfitting.
ex: The {{validation}} set guided our choice of learning rate.

def: When a model learns training noise and fails to generalise to new data.
ex: Heavy {{overfitting}} caused excellent training scores but poor test results.

def: When a model is too simple to capture underlying patterns in the data.
ex: The linear model showed {{underfitting}}, missing the data's clear curvature.

def: A supervised task that predicts a continuous numeric value from input features.
ex: We used linear {{regression}} to forecast next quarter's revenue.

def: A supervised task that assigns inputs to discrete predefined categories or classes.
ex: Email spam {{classification}} sorts messages into spam or not spam.

def: An unsupervised task that groups similar data points without predefined labels.
ex: K-means {{clustering}} revealed three distinct customer segments.

def: A model of interconnected layered nodes inspired by biological brain structure.
ex: A convolutional {{neural network}} achieved high accuracy on image recognition.

def: Machine learning using multi-layered neural networks to learn complex representations.
ex: {{deep learning}} now powers most modern speech recognition systems.

def: Building systems that learn patterns from data rather than explicit programming.
ex: {{machine learning}} lets the system improve automatically as more data arrives.

def: Learning a mapping from inputs to outputs using labelled training examples.
ex: {{supervised learning}} requires each training sample to have a known label.

def: Discovering structure or patterns in data without any labelled outputs.
ex: {{unsupervised learning}} grouped the documents by topic without labels.

def: Training an agent to act by rewarding desirable behaviour through trial and error.
ex: {{reinforcement learning}} taught the agent to master the game through rewards.

def: An optimisation algorithm that iteratively minimises loss by following the gradient.
ex: {{gradient descent}} gradually reduced the loss with each training iteration.

def: A measure quantifying the error between predicted and actual values during training.
ex: Minimising the {{loss function}} drives the optimisation of model parameters.

def: The proportion of correct predictions among all predictions made by a model.
ex: The classifier reached ninety-five percent {{accuracy}} on the test set.

def: The fraction of positive predictions that are actually correct.
ex: High {{precision}} meant few of the flagged transactions were false alarms.

def: The fraction of actual positive cases correctly identified by the model.
ex: The fraud detector prioritised {{recall}} to catch as many cases as possible.

def: The harmonic mean of precision and recall, balancing both metrics into one.
ex: We compared models using the {{F1 score}} to balance precision and recall.

def: A table summarising correct and incorrect predictions per class for a classifier.
ex: The {{confusion matrix}} showed most errors were between two similar classes.

def: A statistical measure of how strongly two variables move together.
ex: There was a strong positive {{correlation}} between advertising and sales.

def: A relationship where one variable directly produces a change in another.
ex: Correlation does not imply {{causation}}, as analysts often warn.

def: A measure of how far data values spread from their mean.
ex: High {{variance}} in the residuals signalled an unstable model.

def: The square root of variance, showing average spread around the mean.
ex: A small {{standard deviation}} indicated the measurements were tightly clustered.

def: The arithmetic average of a set of numeric values.
ex: The {{mean}} income was skewed upward by a few very high earners.

def: The middle value separating the higher and lower halves of ordered data.
ex: We reported the {{median}} salary because outliers distorted the mean.

def: A data point that differs markedly from the rest of the observations.
ex: We removed one extreme {{outlier}} that skewed the regression line.

def: Rescaling numeric features to a common range for consistent model input.
ex: Feature {{normalization}} sped up convergence during gradient descent.

def: Reducing the number of input features while preserving essential information.
ex: {{dimensionality reduction}} compressed hundreds of features into a few components.

def: A technique projecting data onto directions of greatest variance for reduction.
ex: We applied {{PCA}} to visualise the high-dimensional data in two dimensions.

def: Creating or transforming input variables to improve model performance.
ex: Careful {{feature engineering}} added ratios that boosted predictive power.

def: A configuration value set before training that controls the learning process.
ex: Tuning the learning rate {{hyperparameter}} greatly affected convergence speed.

def: Splitting data into folds to evaluate model performance more reliably.
ex: Five-fold {{cross-validation}} gave a robust estimate of accuracy.

def: Systematic error causing a model to consistently miss the true relationship.
ex: A high-{{bias}} model oversimplified the data and underfitted badly.

def: Selecting a subset of data from a larger population for analysis.
ex: Random {{sampling}} ensured the survey represented the whole population.

def: A function describing how probabilities or frequencies spread across values.
ex: The data followed a normal {{distribution}} centred on the mean.

def: A testable statement proposed for evaluation through statistical analysis.
ex: We tested the null {{hypothesis}} that the two groups were identical.

def: The probability of observing results as extreme assuming the null hypothesis.
ex: A {{p-value}} below 0.05 led us to reject the null hypothesis.

def: An automated sequence of steps moving and transforming data between systems.
ex: The {{data pipeline}} ingested, cleaned, and stored records every night.

def: Detecting and correcting errors, duplicates, and inconsistencies in raw data.
ex: {{data cleaning}} removed duplicate rows and fixed inconsistent formats.

def: An absent entry in a dataset where data was not recorded.
ex: We imputed each {{missing value}} using the column's median.

def: A variable taking discrete label values rather than continuous numbers.
ex: The {{categorical variable}} colour was encoded into numeric dummy columns.

def: Datasets too large or complex for traditional processing tools to handle.
ex: {{big data}} platforms let the firm analyse billions of daily events.

def: A central repository storing structured data optimised for analysis and reporting.
ex: Sales figures were loaded into the {{data warehouse}} for quarterly reporting.

def: A storage system holding raw structured and unstructured data at scale.
ex: Raw logs streamed directly into the {{data lake}} for later processing.

def: A process to extract, transform, and load data into a destination system.
ex: The nightly {{ETL}} job moved cleaned records into the warehouse.

def: Representing data graphically to reveal patterns, trends, and insights.
ex: The {{visualization}} made the seasonal sales trend immediately obvious.

def: An interactive display consolidating key metrics and charts for monitoring.
ex: The executive {{dashboard}} tracked revenue and churn in real time.

def: A model that uses historical data to forecast future or unknown outcomes.
ex: The {{predictive model}} estimated which customers would likely cancel.

def: Using a trained model to generate predictions on new input data.
ex: During {{inference}}, the deployed model scored incoming requests instantly.

def: A method combining multiple models to improve overall predictive performance.
ex: An {{ensemble}} of decision trees outperformed any single model.

def: An ensemble of decision trees whose predictions are aggregated together.
ex: The {{random forest}} handled noisy features better than a single tree.

def: A model splitting data by feature rules into branching decision paths.
ex: The {{decision tree}} split customers by age and income to predict churn.

def: The field enabling computers to understand and generate human language.
ex: {{natural language processing}} powered the chatbot's ability to answer queries.

def: Splitting text into smaller units such as words or subwords.
ex: {{tokenization}} broke each sentence into individual word tokens for modelling.

def: A dense numeric vector representing words or items in continuous space.
ex: The word {{embedding}} placed semantically similar terms close together.

def: Discovering useful patterns and relationships within large datasets.
ex: {{data mining}} uncovered surprising buying patterns among loyal customers.

def: The likelihood that an observed result is not due to chance.
ex: The improvement reached {{statistical significance}} at the five percent level.

def: Replacing missing data with substituted estimated values for analysis.
ex: Mean {{imputation}} filled the gaps left by unrecorded measurements.

def: A chart displaying values of two variables as points on axes.
ex: The {{scatter plot}} revealed a clear linear trend between the variables.

def: A sequence of data points ordered and indexed by time.
ex: The {{time series}} of daily sales showed strong weekly seasonality.

def: The complete set of all items or individuals under statistical study.
ex: We estimated the mean height of the entire {{population}} from a small sample.

def: The number of observations included in a statistical sample.
ex: A larger {{sample size}} generally reduces the variance of the estimate.

def: Selecting observations so every member has an equal chance of inclusion.
ex: We used {{random sampling}} to avoid selection bias in the survey.

def: Dividing a population into subgroups and sampling proportionally from each.
ex: {{Stratified sampling}} ensured every age group was represented fairly.

def: A numerical measure of how likely an event is to occur.
ex: The model outputs a {{probability}} between zero and one for each class.

def: A function describing the likelihood of each possible outcome of a variable.
ex: The {{probability distribution}} of the data was clearly right-skewed.

def: A symmetric bell-shaped distribution defined by its mean and standard deviation.
ex: The residuals followed a {{normal distribution}}, satisfying the regression assumption.

def: The distribution of successes in a fixed number of independent trials.
ex: Coin-flip counts follow a {{binomial distribution}} with parameters n and p.

def: Sample means approach a normal distribution as sample size grows large.
ex: Thanks to the {{central limit theorem}}, we assumed normality of the means.

def: A range likely to contain the true parameter with stated confidence.
ex: The 95% {{confidence interval}} for the mean was 4.2 to 5.8.

def: The maximum expected difference between an estimate and the true value.
ex: The poll reported a {{margin of error}} of plus or minus three percent.

def: A default claim of no effect or no difference being tested.
ex: We rejected the {{null hypothesis}} because the p-value was below 0.05.

def: The claim of a real effect accepted if the null is rejected.
ex: The {{alternative hypothesis}} stated that the new drug improves recovery.

def: Wrongly rejecting a true null hypothesis; a false positive.
ex: Lowering the significance level reduces the chance of a {{type I error}}.

def: Failing to reject a false null hypothesis; a false negative.
ex: A small sample increased the risk of a {{type II error}}.

def: The probability a test correctly detects a true effect.
ex: We increased {{statistical power}} by enlarging the study's sample size.

def: The number of independent values free to vary in a calculation.
ex: The t-test used ten {{degrees of freedom}} for its critical value.

def: A test assessing association between categorical variables using observed frequencies.
ex: A {{chi-square test}} confirmed the variables were statistically dependent.

def: A test comparing the means of one or two groups.
ex: A paired {{t-test}} showed a significant difference between the treatments.

def: A method comparing means across three or more groups simultaneously.
ex: One-way {{ANOVA}} revealed significant differences among the four fertilizer groups.

def: A measure of how two variables change together jointly.
ex: A positive {{covariance}} indicated the two features increased together.

def: A standardized number measuring the strength of a linear relationship.
ex: The {{correlation coefficient}} of 0.9 showed a strong positive relationship.

def: Modeling a target as a linear combination of input features.
ex: We fit a {{linear regression}} to predict house prices from area.

def: A model predicting the probability of a binary categorical outcome.
ex: {{Logistic regression}} classified emails as spam or not spam.

def: Regression fitting a curved relationship using polynomial terms of features.
ex: {{Polynomial regression}} captured the nonlinear trend the straight line missed.

def: Linear regression penalizing large coefficients using an L2 penalty term.
ex: {{Ridge regression}} shrank the coefficients and reduced overfitting.

def: Regression using an L1 penalty that can zero out coefficients.
ex: {{Lasso regression}} performed feature selection by eliminating weak predictors.

def: Adding a penalty to model complexity to prevent overfitting.
ex: Applying {{regularization}} improved the model's performance on unseen data.

def: An ensemble building trees sequentially to correct prior errors.
ex: {{Gradient boosting}} outperformed the single decision tree on the benchmark.

def: An optimized, scalable implementation of gradient boosting for structured data.
ex: {{XGBoost}} won the competition thanks to its speed and accuracy.

def: Classifying a point by the majority label of nearest neighbors.
ex: {{K-nearest neighbors}} labeled each sample using its five closest points.

def: A clustering algorithm partitioning data into k groups by centroids.
ex: {{K-means}} grouped the customers into four distinct market segments.

def: Building nested clusters represented as a tree-like dendrogram structure.
ex: {{Hierarchical clustering}} produced a dendrogram showing the nested groupings.

def: A density-based clustering algorithm that also identifies noise points.
ex: {{DBSCAN}} found arbitrarily shaped clusters and flagged outliers as noise.

def: A classifier applying Bayes' theorem assuming feature independence.
ex: {{Naive Bayes}} classified documents quickly despite its independence assumption.

def: Choosing the most relevant input variables for a model.
ex: {{Feature selection}} removed redundant columns and simplified the final model.

def: Transforming features to a common range or scale.
ex: {{Feature scaling}} was essential before applying the distance-based algorithm.

def: Representing categories as binary columns with a single active bit.
ex: {{One-hot encoding}} turned the color column into three binary features.

def: Converting categorical labels into ordered integer values.
ex: {{Label encoding}} mapped each category to a unique integer code.

ru: разделение на обучающую и тестовую выборки
def: Partitioning data into separate sets for training and evaluation.
ex: We used an 80/20 {{train-test split}} to evaluate the model fairly.

def: Splitting data into k folds to validate on each rotationally.
ex: {{K-fold cross-validation}} gave a more reliable estimate of model accuracy.

def: The balance between underfitting error and overfitting error in models.
ex: The {{bias-variance tradeoff}} explained why the complex model generalized poorly.

def: A hyperparameter controlling the step size during model optimization.
ex: A high {{learning rate}} caused the loss to diverge during training.

def: One complete pass of the training data through the model.
ex: The network's accuracy improved steadily after each training {{epoch}}.

def: The number of samples processed before updating model weights.
ex: A smaller {{batch size}} added noise but improved generalization.

def: A nonlinear function applied to a neuron's weighted input.
ex: The ReLU {{activation function}} introduced nonlinearity into the network.

def: An algorithm computing gradients to update neural network weights.
ex: {{Backpropagation}} propagated the loss gradients back through every layer.

def: A network using convolution layers, effective for image data.
ex: A {{convolutional neural network}} classified the medical images with high accuracy.

def: A network with loops suited to sequential and time-series data.
ex: A {{recurrent neural network}} modeled the temporal patterns in the text.

def: A recurrent unit using gates to capture long-range dependencies.
ex: {{Long short-term memory}} networks handled the long sequences without vanishing gradients.

def: An architecture using self-attention to process sequences in parallel.
ex: The {{transformer}} architecture revolutionized modern natural language processing.

def: A method weighting input parts by their relevance to output.
ex: The {{attention mechanism}} let the model focus on relevant words.

def: Reusing a pretrained model's knowledge for a new task.
ex: {{Transfer learning}} let us train an accurate model with little data.

def: Adapting a pretrained model by further training on task data.
ex: {{Fine-tuning}} the language model improved its performance on our domain.

def: A model learning the data distribution to generate new samples.
ex: The {{generative model}} produced realistic synthetic images from noise.

def: A model learning decision boundaries between classes directly.
ex: The {{discriminative model}} focused on separating the two classes accurately.

def: Learning from a mix of labeled and unlabeled data.
ex: {{Semi-supervised learning}} leveraged the many unlabeled examples we had.

def: Identifying rare observations that deviate from expected patterns.
ex: {{Anomaly detection}} flagged the fraudulent transactions in real time.

def: A system suggesting relevant items based on user preferences.
ex: The {{recommendation system}} suggested movies based on viewing history.

def: Recommending items using patterns from similar users' behavior.
ex: {{Collaborative filtering}} recommended products liked by similar shoppers.

def: A plot of true positive against false positive rates.
ex: The {{ROC curve}} showed the classifier's tradeoff across thresholds.

def: The area under the ROC curve summarizing classifier performance.
ex: An {{AUC}} of 0.95 indicated excellent discriminative performance.

def: A metric measuring how well points fit their clusters.
ex: A high {{silhouette score}} confirmed the clusters were well separated.

def: The average of squared differences between predicted and actual values.
ex: We minimized the {{mean squared error}} during regression training.

def: The square root of mean squared error, in original units.
ex: The {{root mean squared error}} was 3.2 dollars per prediction.

def: The proportion of variance in the target explained by predictors.
ex: An {{R-squared}} of 0.85 meant the model explained most variance.

def: Techniques stopping a model from memorizing the training data.
ex: Regularization and dropout are common methods for {{overfitting prevention}}.

def: Randomly disabling neurons during training to reduce overfitting.
ex: Adding {{dropout}} layers reduced overfitting in the deep network.

def: Setting a network's starting weights before training begins.
ex: Proper {{weight initialization}} helped the network converge faster.

def: The process of importing data from various sources into a storage system.
ex: The {{data ingestion}} layer pulls events from Kafka into the warehouse every minute.

def: Converting data from one format or structure into another for analysis.
ex: The {{data transformation}} step cleans nulls and casts columns to correct types.

def: Designing the structure and relationships of data within a system.
ex: Good {{data modeling}} reduced query time by defining clear entity relationships.

def: The formal structure defining tables, columns, and types in a database.
ex: The new {{schema}} adds a customer_id column to every event table.

def: A database storing data in tables linked by defined relationships.
ex: We migrated the orders table into a {{relational database}} to enforce foreign keys.

def: A non-relational database designed for flexible, scalable, schema-less storage.
ex: We chose a {{NoSQL database}} to store variable JSON documents per user session.

def: A subset of a data warehouse focused on a specific business area.
ex: The marketing team queries its own {{data mart}} for campaign metrics.

def: Online Analytical Processing for fast multidimensional queries over large datasets.
ex: The {{OLAP}} cube lets analysts slice revenue by region and quarter instantly.

def: Online Transaction Processing for handling many short, real-time operations.
ex: The {{OLTP}} system records thousands of payment inserts per second.

def: A data model with a central fact table linked to dimension tables.
ex: The {{star schema}} joins the sales fact table to product and date dimensions.

def: A normalized data model where dimension tables branch into sub-dimensions.
ex: The {{snowflake schema}} splits the product dimension into category and brand tables.

def: A column uniquely identifying each row within a database table.
ex: The user_id serves as the {{primary key}} for the customers table.

def: A column referencing the primary key of another related table.
ex: The order table's customer_id is a {{foreign key}} pointing to the customers table.

def: Adding redundant data to a schema to improve read query performance.
ex: We used {{denormalization}} to store the product name directly in the orders table.

def: Splitting a large table into smaller segments for faster access.
ex: Date {{partitioning}} lets queries scan only the relevant daily segments.

def: Distributing data across multiple servers to scale horizontally.
ex: {{Sharding}} the users table by region spread load across five database nodes.

def: Copying data across servers to improve availability and fault tolerance.
ex: Read {{replication}} sends analytics queries to a copy, sparing the primary database.

def: Processing large volumes of data in scheduled groups rather than continuously.
ex: Nightly {{batch processing}} aggregates the day's transactions into summary tables.

def: Continuously processing data records as they arrive in real time.
ex: {{Stream processing}} flags fraudulent transactions within milliseconds of arrival.

def: Analyzing data immediately as it is generated to support instant decisions.
ex: {{Real-time analytics}} updates the live dashboard as each order is placed.

def: The framework of policies ensuring data quality, security, and compliance.
ex: Strong {{data governance}} defines who may access sensitive customer records.

def: The traceable path showing how data moves and transforms over time.
ex: {{Data lineage}} revealed that the revenue metric came from three joined tables.

def: Data describing other data, such as its source, type, or timestamp.
ex: The {{metadata}} records when each table was last refreshed and by whom.

def: An organized inventory of data assets with searchable descriptions and metadata.
ex: Analysts search the {{data catalog}} to find the certified sales dataset.

def: Core reference data shared consistently across an organization's systems.
ex: The {{master data}} keeps one authoritative record per customer across all apps.

def: The degree to which data is accurate, complete, and fit for use.
ex: Poor {{data quality}} caused duplicate emails to skew the campaign report.

def: Checking that data meets defined rules and constraints before use.
ex: {{Data validation}} rejects any row where the age field is negative.

def: Examining data to summarize its structure, content, and quality patterns.
ex: {{Data profiling}} showed that thirty percent of phone numbers were missing.

def: Removing duplicate records to keep a single accurate copy of data.
ex: {{Deduplication}} merged two profiles that shared the same email address.

def: Combining multiple data points into summary values like sums or averages.
ex: The {{aggregation}} computes total revenue grouped by month and region.

def: An operation combining rows from two tables based on a related column.
ex: An inner {{join}} matches orders to customers on the shared customer_id.

def: A table that summarizes data by rotating rows into aggregated columns.
ex: The {{pivot table}} shows sales by product across each month as columns.

def: A SQL function computing values across a set of related rows.
ex: A {{window function}} calculates each customer's running total of purchases.

def: Technologies and practices for analyzing data to support business decisions.
ex: The {{business intelligence}} team built dashboards tracking weekly churn rates.

def: A descriptive attribute used to categorize and filter measured facts.
ex: The time {{dimension}} lets analysts break revenue down by year and month.

def: A numeric value being analyzed, such as revenue, count, or profit.
ex: Total sales is the key {{measure}} tracked across every regional dimension.

def: A central table storing measurable events linked to dimension tables.
ex: The sales {{fact table}} holds one row per transaction with amount and keys.

def: Navigating from summary data to more detailed lower-level records.
ex: A {{drill-down}} from yearly totals reveals sales for each individual store.

def: Aggregating detailed data into higher-level summary totals.
ex: A {{roll-up}} sums daily figures into a single quarterly revenue total.

def: A chart using color intensity to represent values in a matrix.
ex: The {{heatmap}} colors busy hours darker to reveal peak website traffic.

def: A chart showing the frequency distribution of a continuous variable.
ex: The {{histogram}} shows most orders fall between twenty and fifty dollars.

def: A chart summarizing a distribution's median, quartiles, and outliers.
ex: The {{box plot}} highlights several outliers above the upper quartile of prices.

def: A chart using rectangular bars to compare values across categories.
ex: The {{bar chart}} compares monthly revenue across all five product lines.

def: A chart connecting data points with lines to show trends over time.
ex: The {{line chart}} tracks daily active users climbing over six months.

def: A table showing correlation coefficients between many pairs of variables.
ex: The {{correlation matrix}} revealed a strong link between price and returns.

def: A structured presentation of analyzed data for stakeholders to review.
ex: The weekly {{report}} summarizes sales, refunds, and customer growth metrics.

def: Studying groups sharing a common trait over time to compare behavior.
ex: {{Cohort analysis}} showed January signups retained better than March ones.

def: Tracking how users progress and drop off through sequential steps.
ex: {{Funnel analysis}} found most users abandon checkout at the payment step.

def: Comparing two variants to determine which performs better statistically.
ex: {{A/B testing}} showed the green button raised sign-ups by eight percent.

def: Methods summarizing a dataset's main features like mean and spread.
ex: {{Descriptive statistics}} report a mean order value of forty-two dollars.

def: Methods drawing conclusions about a population from a data sample.
ex: {{Inferential statistics}} estimated national demand from a survey of buyers.

def: Investigating a dataset with visuals and summaries to find patterns.
ex: {{Exploratory data analysis}} uncovered an unexpected seasonal spike in returns.

def: Communicating insights through a compelling narrative built on data.
ex: Good {{data storytelling}} turned the churn report into a clear action plan.

def: Using historical data and models to forecast future outcomes.
ex: {{Predictive analytics}} forecasts which customers are likely to churn next month.

def: Recommending specific actions based on data and predicted outcomes.
ex: {{Prescriptive analytics}} suggested the optimal discount to maximize total profit.

def: Data organized into a fixed format like rows and columns.
ex: {{Structured data}} in the sales table fits neatly into defined columns.

def: Data without a predefined format, such as text, images, or audio.
ex: Customer reviews are {{unstructured data}} requiring text mining to analyze.

def: Data with some organizational tags but no rigid tabular schema.
ex: JSON logs are {{semi-structured data}} with flexible nested key-value fields.

def: A value dividing ranked data into four equal-sized groups.
ex: The top {{quartile}} of customers generates half of total annual revenue.

def: A value below which a given percentage of observations fall.
ex: A response time in the 95th {{percentile}} means only five percent are slower.

def: A measure of how asymmetric a distribution is around its mean.
ex: Positive {{skewness}} shows a long tail of high-value outlier purchases.

def: A measure of how heavy-tailed or peaked a distribution is.
ex: High {{kurtosis}} indicates frequent extreme values in the returns data.

def: An architecture combining data lake storage with warehouse query features.
ex: The {{data lakehouse}} stores raw files yet supports fast SQL analytics.

def: Storing table data by column to speed up analytical queries.
ex: {{Columnar storage}} lets the query scan only the two needed columns.

def: Coordinating and scheduling the steps of automated data workflows.
ex: {{Data pipeline orchestration}} in Airflow runs ingestion before the nightly transforms.

def: Loading only new or changed records rather than the entire dataset.
ex: An {{incremental load}} adds only yesterday's orders instead of reloading everything.

def: A formal set of rules directing how data is managed and protected.
ex: The {{data governance policy}} requires masking all personal identifiers in reports.

def: A measurable value showing how well a goal is being achieved.
ex: Monthly active users is the main {{key performance indicator}} for the product team.

def: A single value summarizing a feature of a dataset, like the mean.
ex: The median is a robust {{summary statistic}} less affected by extreme outliers.

def: The simplest artificial neuron computing a weighted sum passed through a threshold.
ex: The single-layer {{perceptron}} can only separate linearly separable classes.

def: A feedforward network with one or more hidden layers of neurons.
ex: A {{multilayer perceptron}} learns nonlinear functions through stacked hidden layers.

def: An intermediate layer between input and output that learns internal representations.
ex: Adding another {{hidden layer}} increased the model's representational capacity.

def: A grid of learnable parameters connecting neurons between two adjacent layers.
ex: The {{weight matrix}} is updated during each backpropagation step.

def: A learnable constant added to a neuron's weighted sum before activation.
ex: The {{bias term}} lets the activation shift independently of the inputs.

def: An activation squashing values into the range between zero and one.
ex: The {{sigmoid}} output is often read as a probability.

def: An activation returning zero for negatives and the input for positives.
ex: {{ReLU}} helps mitigate vanishing gradients in deep networks.

def: A function turning a vector of scores into a probability distribution.
ex: The final {{softmax}} assigns a probability to each class.

def: When gradients shrink toward zero, stalling learning in early layers.
ex: The {{vanishing gradient}} problem plagued very deep sigmoid networks.

def: When gradients grow uncontrollably large, destabilizing the training process.
ex: Gradient clipping is a common remedy for the {{exploding gradient}}.

def: A technique normalizing layer inputs across a mini-batch to speed training.
ex: We add {{batch normalization}} to use higher learning rates safely.

def: A layer downsampling feature maps to reduce spatial dimensions.
ex: A max {{pooling layer}} keeps the strongest activation per region.

def: The step size a filter moves across the input during convolution.
ex: A larger {{stride}} produces a smaller output feature map.

def: A small matrix slid over an input to compute convolutions.
ex: A three-by-three {{kernel}} detects local edges in the image.

def: A learnable kernel that extracts a specific pattern from inputs.
ex: Each {{filter}} learns to respond to a distinct visual feature.

def: The output produced when a filter is convolved over an input.
ex: Each {{feature map}} highlights where a pattern appears.

def: A network trained to reconstruct its input through a compressed bottleneck.
ex: The {{autoencoder}} learns a compact code of the data.

def: Two networks competing so a generator produces realistic synthetic samples.
ex: A {{generative adversarial network}} can synthesize photorealistic faces.

def: A component mapping input into a compressed internal representation.
ex: The {{encoder}} compresses the sentence into a context vector.

def: A component reconstructing output from an internal representation.
ex: The {{decoder}} generates the translated sentence token by token.

def: A layer mapping discrete tokens to dense continuous vectors.
ex: The {{embedding layer}} converts word indices into learned vectors.

def: Vectors injecting token order information into an attention-based model.
ex: The {{positional encoding}} tells the transformer where each token sits.

def: A mechanism letting each token attend to all others in a sequence.
ex: Using {{self-attention}} lets the model relate distant words directly.

def: Capping gradient magnitude to prevent unstable updates during training.
ex: Adding {{gradient clipping}} keeps recurrent training from diverging.

def: A penalty shrinking weights to reduce overfitting during optimization.
ex: Adding {{weight decay}} improved generalization on the test set.

def: An algorithm updating model parameters to minimize the loss.
ex: We switched the {{optimizer}} from SGD to Adam.

def: An adaptive optimizer combining momentum with per-parameter learning rates.
ex: {{Adam}} converges quickly with little tuning effort.

def: An optimizer term accumulating past gradients to smooth updates.
ex: Adding {{momentum}} helped the optimizer roll through flat regions.

def: An activation squashing values into the range minus one to one.
ex: The {{tanh}} activation is zero-centered, unlike the sigmoid.

def: A ReLU variant allowing a small slope for negative inputs.
ex: The {{leaky ReLU}} keeps neurons from dying on negative values.

def: A layer where every input connects to every output neuron.
ex: The final {{fully connected layer}} maps features to class scores.

def: The final layer producing the network's predictions.
ex: The {{output layer}} uses softmax for multiclass classification.

def: The first layer receiving raw feature values into the network.
ex: The {{input layer}} has one node per feature.

def: Computing outputs by propagating inputs forward through the network.
ex: During the {{forward pass}} we compute the loss.

def: Propagating gradients backward to compute parameter updates.
ex: The {{backward pass}} distributes error to each weight.

def: A directed graph representing operations and dependencies in a model.
ex: Autograd builds a {{computational graph}} to track gradients.

def: A multidimensional array holding data flowing through a network.
ex: The image batch is stored as a four-dimensional {{tensor}}.

def: A small subset of samples processed together in one update.
ex: Each {{mini-batch}} yields one gradient update.

def: Optimization updating parameters using gradients from random sample subsets.
ex: Plain {{stochastic gradient descent}} scales well to huge datasets.

def: An optimizer scaling updates by a running average of squared gradients.
ex: {{RMSprop}} adapts each parameter's effective learning rate.

def: An optimizer adapting learning rates using accumulated squared gradients.
ex: {{Adagrad}} suits sparse features but decays rates aggressively.

def: A loss measuring divergence between predicted and true class distributions.
ex: The {{cross-entropy loss}} is standard for classification tasks.

def: Softening target labels slightly to discourage overconfident predictions.
ex: Adding {{label smoothing}} improved calibration on the classifier.

def: A shortcut passing activations directly to a deeper layer.
ex: A {{skip connection}} eases gradient flow in deep nets.

def: A skip that adds a layer's input to its output.
ex: The {{residual connection}} lets the block learn a residual.

def: A deep architecture built from stacked residual blocks.
ex: A {{residual network}} can train hundreds of layers deep.

def: Normalizing activations across features within a single training example.
ex: We use {{layer normalization}} to stabilize training in transformer blocks.

def: Running several attention operations in parallel over different subspaces.
ex: With {{multi-head attention}} the model captures diverse patterns at once.

def: One parallel attention subunit within a multi-head layer.
ex: Each {{attention head}} focuses on a different relationship.

def: The vector representing what a token seeks in attention.
ex: The {{query vector}} is matched against every key.

def: The vector a query is compared against to score relevance.
ex: Attention weights come from query and {{key vector}} dot products.

def: The content vector aggregated according to attention weights.
ex: The output blends each {{value vector}} by its weight.

def: Feeding ground-truth tokens as inputs during sequence model training.
ex: Using {{teacher forcing}} speeds convergence but can cause exposure bias.

def: A decoding heuristic keeping the top candidate sequences at each step.
ex: Applying {{beam search}} finds higher-quality translations than greedy decoding.

def: Choosing the single most probable token at each generation step.
ex: Fast {{greedy decoding}} can still miss better sequences.

def: An architecture mapping an input sequence to an output sequence.
ex: A {{sequence-to-sequence}} model powers machine translation.

def: A generative autoencoder learning a probabilistic latent distribution.
ex: A {{variational autoencoder}} samples new data from latent space.

def: A compressed continuous space encoding meaningful data representations.
ex: Interpolating in {{latent space}} morphs one face into another.

def: The GAN component producing synthetic samples from noise.
ex: The {{generator network}} learns to fool the discriminator.

def: The GAN component judging whether samples are real or fake.
ex: The {{discriminator network}} scores each image's authenticity.

def: When a generator outputs limited variety, ignoring parts of the distribution.
ex: Severe {{mode collapse}} left the GAN producing nearly identical faces.

def: An input perturbed subtly to fool a trained model.
ex: An {{adversarial example}} flips the classifier with tiny noise.

def: A convolution with gaps that enlarge the receptive field.
ex: A {{dilated convolution}} widens context without extra parameters.

def: A convolution that upsamples inputs to larger spatial sizes.
ex: The decoder uses {{transposed convolution}} to enlarge feature maps.

def: The input region influencing a particular neuron's activation.
ex: Deeper layers have a larger {{receptive field}}.

def: Adding border values so convolutions preserve spatial dimensions.
ex: Zero {{padding}} keeps the output the same size.

def: One depth slice of a feature map or image.
ex: An RGB image has three color {{channel}}s.

def: A visualization showing which input regions drive a prediction.
ex: The {{saliency map}} highlighted the pixels the model used.

def: Halting training when validation performance stops improving.
ex: Using {{early stopping}} prevented the network from overfitting.

def: A rule adjusting the learning rate over the course of training.
ex: A decaying {{learning rate schedule}} refined the final weights.

def: Setting initial weights to keep signal variance stable across layers.
ex: {{Xavier initialization}} suits tanh and sigmoid networks.

def: A weight initialization scaled for ReLU-based activation functions.
ex: {{He initialization}} keeps variance healthy through ReLU layers.

def: Training a small model to mimic a larger teacher network.
ex: Applying {{knowledge distillation}} compressed the model with little accuracy loss.

def: Removing unimportant weights or neurons to shrink a network.
ex: Aggressive {{pruning}} cut the model size by half.

def: Reducing numerical precision of weights to speed and compress models.
ex: Int8 {{quantization}} let the model run on a mobile chip.

def: Reducing words to their base dictionary form using morphological analysis of language.
ex: Applying {{lemmatization}} converts "running" and "ran" into the base word "run".

def: Chopping word endings to obtain a rough root form for text processing.
ex: Simple {{stemming}} strips suffixes, turning "fishing" and "fished" into "fish".

def: A very common word removed from text before analysis to reduce noise.
ex: Filtering each {{stop word}} like "the" and "is" shrinks the vocabulary.

def: A text representation counting word occurrences while ignoring grammar and order.
ex: The {{bag of words}} model represents a document as a vector of word counts.

def: A weighting scheme scoring words by frequency and rarity across documents.
ex: Using {{TF-IDF}} downweights common words and highlights terms unique to a document.

def: A dense vector capturing a word's meaning learned from surrounding context.
ex: A trained {{word embedding}} places "king" and "queen" close together in vector space.

def: Identifying and classifying names of people, places, and organizations in text.
ex: The {{named entity recognition}} model tagged "Paris" as a location in the sentence.

def: Labelling each word with its grammatical category such as noun or verb.
ex: Accurate {{part-of-speech tagging}} marks "run" as a verb in that clause.

def: Detecting the emotional polarity, positive or negative, expressed in a piece of text.
ex: Running {{sentiment analysis}} on reviews shows most customers feel positive about the product.

def: A model that predicts the probability of word sequences in a language.
ex: A large {{language model}} predicts the next word given the preceding context.

def: A large structured collection of texts used for linguistic analysis and training.
ex: The team trained the model on a {{corpus}} of millions of news articles.

def: A contiguous sequence of n items, usually words, taken from text.
ex: A bigram is an {{n-gram}} of two words such as "machine learning".

def: A tool that splits raw text into smaller units called tokens.
ex: The {{tokenizer}} broke the sentence into individual words and punctuation marks.

def: Assigning a single category label to an entire input image.
ex: The {{image classification}} network labelled each photo as either a cat or dog.

def: Locating and classifying multiple objects within an image using bounding regions.
ex: The {{object detection}} system drew boxes around every car in the street scene.

def: Assigning a class label to every pixel in an image.
ex: With {{semantic segmentation}}, each pixel of the road was labelled separately from the sky.

def: A rectangle marking the location and extent of an object in an image.
ex: Each detected face was enclosed in a tight {{bounding box}} with coordinates.

def: A sliding filter operation extracting local patterns from image data.
ex: Each {{convolution}} slides a small kernel across the image to detect edges.

def: Deriving informative numerical attributes from raw data for a model.
ex: Automatic {{feature extraction}} converts raw pixels into edges and shapes for classification.

def: Converting images of printed or handwritten text into machine-readable characters.
ex: Using {{optical character recognition}}, the scanner turned the paper form into editable text.

def: Creating new training samples by transforming existing data to improve generalization.
ex: Through {{data augmentation}}, each image was flipped and rotated to expand the dataset.

def: Manually labelling images with tags, boxes, or masks for training.
ex: Careful {{image annotation}} provided the ground-truth labels the detector needed to learn.

def: The brightness value of a single pixel, typically ranging from zero to 255.
ex: High {{pixel intensity}} in that region indicated a bright spot in the image.

def: A method learning word embeddings by predicting context words from a target.
ex: The classic {{word2vec}} algorithm learns vectors so similar words cluster together.

def: A measure of similarity between vectors based on the angle between them.
ex: Computing {{cosine similarity}} between embeddings shows how semantically close two words are.

def: Assigning predefined category labels to documents or sentences automatically.
ex: A {{text classification}} model routes each incoming email into spam or inbox.

def: Discovering abstract themes that occur across a collection of documents.
ex: Applying {{topic modeling}} revealed hidden themes spanning thousands of research abstracts.

def: A probabilistic model representing documents as mixtures of latent topics.
ex: Using {{latent Dirichlet allocation}}, each document was described as a blend of topics.

def: Automatically converting text from one natural language into another.
ex: Neural {{machine translation}} rendered the English article into fluent Kazakh instantly.

def: Producing a shorter version of a text preserving its key information.
ex: Automatic {{text summarization}} condensed the long report into three key sentences.

def: A task where a model returns a precise answer to a natural-language question.
ex: The {{question answering}} system extracted the exact date from the passage.

def: Splitting words into frequent fragments to handle rare and unknown terms.
ex: With {{subword tokenization}}, "unhappiness" is broken into "un", "happi", and "ness".

def: A tokenization method merging frequent character pairs into subword units.
ex: {{Byte pair encoding}} builds a vocabulary by repeatedly merging the commonest symbol pairs.

def: A metric measuring how well a language model predicts a text sample.
ex: A lower {{perplexity}} means the language model predicts the held-out text more confidently.

def: A metric comparing machine translation output against reference human translations.
ex: The system scored a high {{BLEU score}}, indicating close overlap with reference translations.

def: The complete set of unique tokens a model recognises and can process.
ex: Words outside the model's {{vocabulary}} are replaced by a special unknown token.

def: Describing a token absent from the model's known vocabulary set.
ex: Rare names are often {{out-of-vocabulary}}, forcing the model to use fallback tokens.

def: Analysing grammatical structure by linking words through directed syntactic relations.
ex: {{Dependency parsing}} connects each verb to its subject and object in the sentence.

def: Determining which words in text refer to the same real-world entity.
ex: {{Coreference resolution}} links "she" back to "Maria" mentioned earlier in the paragraph.

def: Standardising text by lowercasing, expanding abbreviations, and cleaning irregular formatting.
ex: {{Text normalization}} converted "U.S.A." and "usa" into a single consistent form.

def: The number of documents in a collection containing a particular term.
ex: A word with high {{document frequency}} appears across most texts and carries little weight.

def: A word vector that changes depending on the surrounding sentence context.
ex: A {{contextual embedding}} gives "bank" different vectors in "river bank" and "savings bank".

def: A structure where one network encodes input and another generates output.
ex: The {{encoder-decoder}} compresses the sentence into a vector, then expands it into a summary.

def: The input text instructing a language model what to generate.
ex: A well-crafted {{prompt}} guides the model to produce a concise, relevant answer.

def: An image format representing each pixel by a single brightness value.
ex: Converting the photo to {{grayscale}} removed colour and kept only intensity values.

def: One of the red, green, or blue layers composing a colour image.
ex: Each {{RGB channel}} stores intensity for one primary colour of the image.

def: Downsampling a feature map by summarising values within local regions.
ex: Max {{pooling}} keeps the strongest activation from each small patch of features.

def: Detecting objects and outlining a separate mask for each individual instance.
ex: {{Instance segmentation}} separates two overlapping people into distinct pixel masks.

def: Partitioning an image into meaningful regions for detailed analysis.
ex: Medical {{image segmentation}} outlines the tumour region within the scan.

def: Identifying boundaries where image brightness changes sharply between regions.
ex: {{Edge detection}} highlighted the outlines of every object in the photograph.

def: Preparing raw images by resizing, cropping, and normalising before model input.
ex: {{Image preprocessing}} resized and normalised every photo to a fixed input format.

def: A metric measuring overlap between a predicted and true bounding box.
ex: A high {{intersection over union}} means the predicted box closely matches the ground truth.

def: A predefined reference rectangle used by detectors to predict object locations.
ex: Each {{anchor box}} of a fixed shape is refined to fit a detected object.

def: Removing overlapping detections and keeping only the highest-scoring bounding box.
ex: {{Non-maximum suppression}} discarded duplicate boxes and kept the most confident detection.

def: Locating distinctive points on an object, such as joints or facial landmarks.
ex: {{Keypoint detection}} marked the elbows and knees for the pose-estimation model.

def: Predicting the spatial configuration of a body or object from an image.
ex: {{Pose estimation}} reconstructed the dancer's skeleton from a single photograph.

def: Identifying or verifying a person from an image of their face.
ex: The phone's {{facial recognition}} unlocked the screen after matching the owner's face.

def: The pattern of apparent motion between consecutive video frames.
ex: {{Optical flow}} tracked how each pixel moved between two successive frames.

def: The number of pixels defining the detail contained in an image.
ex: Higher {{image resolution}} preserves fine details but requires more memory to process.

def: The proportional relationship between an image's width and its height.
ex: Resizing changed the {{aspect ratio}}, stretching the objects in the photo.

def: Enhancing image contrast by redistributing pixel intensity values more evenly.
ex: {{Histogram equalization}} brightened the dark photo and revealed hidden detail.

def: Assigning meaningful tags to raw data to create training examples.
ex: Accurate {{data labeling}} gave the classifier reliable ground-truth categories to learn.

def: The verified correct labels against which model predictions are compared.
ex: The model's output was checked against the {{ground truth}} to measure accuracy.

def: The stack of self-attention layers turning input tokens into contextual representations.
ex: The {{transformer encoder}} processes all tokens at once to build rich context vectors.

def: A pretraining task where the model predicts hidden words in text.
ex: During {{masked language modeling}}, the model guesses the missing word from context.

def: Producing coherent natural-language text automatically from a model.
ex: {{Text generation}} let the assistant write a full paragraph from a short prompt.

def: Studying the internal structure of words and their meaningful components.
ex: {{Morphological analysis}} splits "unbreakable" into the prefix, root, and suffix.

def: Adding linguistic labels to texts in a corpus for training or study.
ex: Careful {{corpus annotation}} tagged each sentence with grammatical and semantic information.

def: A word2vec variant predicting surrounding context words from a target word.
ex: The {{skip-gram}} model learns embeddings by predicting neighbours of each centre word.

def: Representing documents and queries as vectors in a shared feature space.
ex: In a {{vector space model}}, similar documents lie close together as neighbouring vectors.

def: Automatically generating a natural-language description of an image's content.
ex: {{Image captioning}} produced the sentence "a dog running on grass" for the photo.

def: A written standard defining how annotators should label data consistently.
ex: Following the {{data annotation guideline}}, every labeler tagged ambiguous cases the same way.

def: Automatically detecting which natural language a piece of text is written in.
ex: {{Language identification}} flagged the comment as Kazakh before routing it for translation.

def: Automatically detecting and correcting misspelled words in written text.
ex: Automatic {{spell checking}} flagged "recieve" and suggested the correct spelling "receive".

def: Removing noise, markup, and duplicates from raw text before training.
ex: {{Text corpus cleaning}} stripped HTML tags and repeated lines from the scraped pages.

def: A learnable weight grid detecting a specific visual pattern in images.
ex: Each {{convolutional filter}} learns to respond to a particular texture or edge.

def: An architecture applying attention to image patches instead of convolutions.
ex: The {{vision transformer}} splits the picture into patches and attends across them.

def: A compact vector summarising an image's content for comparison or search.
ex: An {{image embedding}} lets the system retrieve visually similar photos quickly.

def: Interpreting the overall context and relationships among objects in an image.
ex: {{Scene understanding}} recognised that the person was crossing a busy street.

def: The process of putting a trained model into a production environment for use.
ex: The team automated {{model deployment}} to reduce manual release errors.

def: A central repository storing versioned models with their metadata and lifecycle stages.
ex: Every trained artifact is logged to the {{model registry}} before promotion.

def: Gradual decline in model performance as real-world data changes over time.
ex: Monitoring dashboards flagged {{model drift}} two weeks after release.

def: A change in the statistical relationship between input features and the target.
ex: Seasonal buying habits caused {{concept drift}} in the demand forecaster.

def: A shift in the distribution of input data compared to training data.
ex: An alert triggered when {{data drift}} exceeded the configured threshold.

def: A system for storing, sharing, and serving curated features for models.
ex: Both training and serving read the same values from the {{feature store}}.

def: Continuously tracking a deployed model's performance, inputs, and predictions.
ex: Robust {{model monitoring}} caught the accuracy drop within hours.

def: An automated workflow that refreshes a model on new data periodically.
ex: The nightly {{retraining pipeline}} keeps the recommender up to date.

def: Tracking and labeling successive states of datasets for reproducibility.
ex: With {{data versioning}}, we can rerun any past experiment exactly.

def: Recording parameters, metrics, and artifacts across model training runs.
ex: Good {{experiment tracking}} lets us compare hundreds of runs quickly.

def: Searching for the parameter values that yield the best model performance.
ex: Careful {{hyperparameter tuning}} raised validation accuracy by four points.

def: Exhaustively evaluating every combination in a defined parameter grid.
ex: A {{grid search}} over three parameters trained ninety models overnight.

def: Sampling random parameter combinations to find good model settings efficiently.
ex: In practice, {{random search}} often beats grid search when only a few parameters matter.

def: Exposing a trained model so applications can request predictions from it.
ex: We use a dedicated {{model serving}} layer behind a load balancer.

def: The time taken for a model to return a prediction per request.
ex: Quantization cut {{inference latency}} nearly in half on mobile.

def: A formal agreement defining the schema and guarantees of shared data.
ex: A broken {{data contract}} stopped the pipeline before bad rows spread.

def: Coordinating dependent tasks into a scheduled, monitored automated pipeline.
ex: Modern {{workflow orchestration}} tools rerun only the tasks that failed.

def: A task graph with directed edges and no cycles defining execution order.
ex: Each pipeline is modeled as a {{directed acyclic graph}} of steps.

def: A task that produces the same result no matter how often it runs.
ex: An {{idempotent job}} can be safely retried after a transient failure.

def: Rerunning a pipeline over historical dates to populate missing outputs.
ex: We scheduled a {{backfill}} to recompute six months of metrics.

def: How recently data was updated relative to when it is consumed.
ex: A staleness alert fires when {{data freshness}} falls below one hour.

def: Managing changes to a data structure without breaking existing consumers.
ex: Backward-compatible {{schema evolution}} let us add a column safely.

def: Assigning unique identifiers to successive iterations of a trained model.
ex: Strict {{model versioning}} let us roll back to last week's release.

def: Automatically building and testing code each time changes are merged.
ex: Our {{continuous integration}} setup runs the full test suite on every commit.

def: Automatically releasing every validated change straight into production.
ex: With {{continuous deployment}}, approved models ship without manual steps.

def: Automatically retraining models on fresh data as part of the pipeline.
ex: A {{continuous training}} loop keeps the fraud model aligned with new patterns.

def: The serialized file produced by training that can be loaded for inference.
ex: The build stores each {{model artifact}} in object storage.

def: Packaging code and dependencies into a portable, isolated runtime unit.
ex: Using {{containerization}} makes the model run identically across environments.

def: A network address where an application sends data to get predictions.
ex: Client apps call the {{model endpoint}} over HTTPS for scoring.

def: Scoring a large collection of records together on a schedule.
ex: Nightly {{batch inference}} scores millions of customers at once.

def: Generating predictions in real time as individual requests arrive.
ex: The checkout page relies on {{online inference}} for instant scores.

def: Running a new model alongside production without affecting user responses.
ex: A {{shadow deployment}} validated the model on live traffic risk-free.

def: Releasing a change to a small user subset before a full rollout.
ex: A {{canary deployment}} exposed the model to five percent of traffic.

def: Switching traffic between two identical environments for zero-downtime releases.
ex: A {{blue-green deployment}} let us cut over instantly and roll back fast.

def: Reverting a system to a previous stable version after a failure.
ex: A one-click {{rollback}} restored the earlier model within seconds.

def: An automated workflow that computes and delivers features for models.
ex: The {{feature pipeline}} refreshes aggregates every fifteen minutes.

def: Detecting and streaming row-level changes from a source database.
ex: Streaming {{change data capture}} keeps the warehouse in sync with the app.

def: A design where components react to and communicate through emitted events.
ex: An {{event-driven architecture}} decouples the producers from consumers.

def: A buffer that passes messages between services asynchronously and reliably.
ex: Ingestion writes events to a {{message queue}} for later processing.

def: A system that runs tasks at defined times or on triggers.
ex: The {{job scheduler}} kicks off the ETL run at midnight daily.

def: A task scheduled to run automatically at fixed recurring intervals.
ex: A {{cron job}} exports the report every morning at seven.

def: A condition or event that automatically starts a pipeline run.
ex: A new file in the bucket acts as the {{pipeline trigger}}.

def: The degree to which a model's decisions can be understood by humans.
ex: Regulators demanded {{model explainability}} for the credit scoring system.

def: A score reflecting how much each input contributes to predictions.
ex: The {{feature importance}} chart showed income dominating the model.

def: The steady loss of predictive quality in a model over time.
ex: Frequent retraining offsets the {{model decay}} we observed monthly.

def: The current best-performing model actively serving production traffic.
ex: The {{champion model}} stays live until a challenger clearly beats it.

def: A candidate model tested against the current production champion.
ex: The {{challenger model}} improved recall enough to earn promotion.

def: Policies and controls governing how models are approved, used, and audited.
ex: Strong {{model governance}} requires sign-off before any model goes live.

def: A document summarizing a model's purpose, performance, and limitations.
ex: Each release ships with a {{model card}} for transparency.

def: A measure of whether a model treats groups equitably in its predictions.
ex: A {{fairness metric}} revealed uneven approval rates across regions.

def: A database recording context about datasets, runs, and models.
ex: The {{metadata store}} links every prediction back to its training run.

def: Converting an object into a storable or transmittable byte format.
ex: Model {{serialization}} lets us save weights to a single file.

def: A saved snapshot of model state during training for later resumption.
ex: Training resumed from the last {{model checkpoint}} after the crash.

def: Poor performance when a system lacks history about new users or items.
ex: The recommender's {{cold start}} problem hurt brand-new sign-ups.

def: Automatically adjusting compute resources to match changing workload demand.
ex: Cluster {{autoscaling}} added replicas as inference traffic spiked at noon.

def: Distributing incoming requests across servers to optimize responsiveness.
ex: Server {{load balancing}} spreads scoring calls evenly over four replicas.

def: The number of requests or records a system processes per unit time.
ex: Batching raised {{throughput}} to ten thousand predictions per second.

def: Training a model across multiple machines or accelerators in parallel.
ex: Switching to {{distributed training}} cut the run from days to a few hours.

def: Splitting data across devices that each hold a full model copy.
ex: Using {{data parallelism}}, each GPU processed a different mini-batch.

def: Splitting a single model across devices when it exceeds one's memory.
ex: With {{model parallelism}}, the huge network spread over eight GPUs.

def: Removing redundant weights or neurons to make a model smaller.
ex: Careful {{model pruning}} dropped forty percent of parameters without harm.

def: Techniques that reduce a model's size and computational cost.
ex: Aggressive {{model compression}} let the network run on a phone.

def: Running models directly on local devices instead of central servers.
ex: An {{edge deployment}} enables offline predictions on the sensor itself.

def: A holding queue for messages that repeatedly fail to be processed.
ex: Malformed events land in the {{dead letter queue}} for later review.

def: A guarantee that each event affects results one time only.
ex: Enabling {{exactly-once processing}} prevents double-counting in the billing stream.

def: A marker in streaming that tracks progress of event time.
ex: The {{watermark}} tells the job when a time window can close.

def: Reprocessing stored past events to rebuild or correct outputs.
ex: A {{data replay}} regenerated the metrics after we fixed the bug.

def: Periodically saving progress so work can resume after a failure.
ex: Stream {{checkpointing}} lets the job restart without losing state.

def: The ability to monitor the health and reliability of data pipelines.
ex: Our {{data observability}} tools alerted us to a silent volume drop.

def: A deployed service that returns model outputs to client applications.
ex: The mobile app queries the {{prediction service}} on every search.

def: A shift over time in the distribution of a model's outputs.
ex: Rising {{prediction drift}} hinted the input data had changed.

def: The ability to obtain the same results by rerunning an experiment.
ex: Pinning seeds and versions ensured full {{reproducibility}} of the run.

def: Initializing training from existing weights rather than from scratch.
ex: A {{warm start}} from last week's model sped up convergence.

def: Method of updating probability estimates as new evidence becomes available.
ex: Analysts used {{Bayesian inference}} to refine the fraud detection model.

def: Initial belief about an outcome before observing any new data.
ex: The {{prior probability}} reflected historical rates before the experiment began.

def: Updated belief about an outcome after incorporating observed evidence.
ex: The {{posterior probability}} rose sharply after the positive test result.

def: Probability of observed data given a particular set of parameters.
ex: The {{likelihood}} measures how well parameters explain the data.

def: Finding parameter values that make observed data most probable.
ex: They fitted the model using {{maximum likelihood estimation}} on the sample.

def: Procedure for deciding whether data support a specific claim.
ex: Careful {{hypothesis testing}} confirmed the new layout increased conversions.

def: Quantitative measure of the magnitude of an observed difference.
ex: A large {{effect size}} showed the treatment mattered practically.

def: Difference between a sample statistic and the true population value.
ex: Small samples suffer from large {{sampling error}} and noisy results.

def: Resampling technique estimating statistics by repeatedly sampling with replacement.
ex: The {{bootstrapping}} procedure produced intervals without distributional assumptions.

def: Drawing repeated samples from data to estimate statistical variability.
ex: These {{resampling}} methods validated the estimator across many pseudo-datasets.

def: Estimating outcomes by running many randomized computational trials.
ex: A {{Monte Carlo simulation}} approximated the portfolio's risk distribution.

def: Tendency of extreme measurements to move toward the average later.
ex: Improvement partly reflected {{regression to the mean}}, not the intervention.

def: High correlation among predictors that distorts regression coefficient estimates.
ex: Severe {{multicollinearity}} inflated the variance of the coefficient estimates.

def: Unequal variance of residuals across levels of a predictor.
ex: Observed {{heteroscedasticity}} violated an assumption of the linear model.

def: Difference between an observed value and its predicted value.
ex: Each {{residual}} showed how far predictions missed the actual data.

def: How well a statistical model matches the observed data.
ex: The chi-square measured the {{goodness of fit}} of the distribution.

def: Spread between the first and third quartiles of data.
ex: The {{interquartile range}} summarized variability while ignoring extreme outliers.

def: Number of standard deviations a value lies from the mean.
ex: A {{z-score}} of three flagged the observation as unusual.

def: Estimated standard deviation of a sample statistic's sampling distribution.
ex: A smaller {{standard error}} meant the estimate was more precise.

def: Participants who do not receive the experimental treatment for comparison.
ex: The {{control group}} kept using the old checkout flow.

def: Participants who receive the intervention being tested in an experiment.
ex: The {{treatment group}} saw the redesigned onboarding screens.

def: Hidden factor that influences both cause and observed outcome.
ex: Age was a {{confounding variable}} distorting the treatment comparison.

def: Experiment randomly assigning subjects to treatment and control groups.
ex: A {{randomized controlled trial}} established the drug's causal effect.

def: Systematic error causing estimates to deviate from true values.
ex: Selection {{statistical bias}} skewed the survey toward loyal customers.

def: Single value used to approximate an unknown population parameter.
ex: The sample mean served as a {{point estimate}} of demand.

def: Range of values likely to contain an unknown parameter.
ex: An {{interval estimate}} conveyed uncertainty better than a single number.

def: Distribution of a statistic across all possible samples drawn.
ex: The {{sampling distribution}} of the mean approached normality.

def: Numerical characteristic describing an entire population's distribution.
ex: The population mean is a {{parameter}} estimated from the sample.

def: Numerical value computed from sample data to estimate parameters.
ex: The sample average is a {{statistic}} summarizing the observations.

def: Rule or formula that produces an estimate from data.
ex: An unbiased {{estimator}} centers on the true parameter value.

def: Property that an estimator converges to the true value asymptotically.
ex: Estimator {{consistency}} guarantees accuracy improves as the sample grows.

def: Threshold probability for rejecting the null hypothesis, often 0.05.
ex: They set the {{significance level}} at five percent beforehand.

def: Test detecting an effect in only one specified direction.
ex: A {{one-tailed test}} checked only whether sales increased.

def: Test detecting an effect in either direction from the null.
ex: A {{two-tailed test}} allowed for both increases and decreases.

def: Threshold a test statistic must exceed to reject null.
ex: The test statistic surpassed the {{critical value}}, rejecting the null.

def: Value computed from data to evaluate a statistical hypothesis.
ex: The {{test statistic}} fell within the rejection region.

def: Distribution modeling counts of rare events over fixed intervals.
ex: Website crashes followed a {{Poisson distribution}} across the month.

def: Distribution where all outcomes have equal probability of occurring.
ex: A fair die follows a {{uniform distribution}} over six faces.

def: Distribution modeling time between independent events occurring continuously.
ex: Wait times between calls fit an {{exponential distribution}}.

def: Function describing relative likelihood of a continuous variable's values.
ex: The {{probability density function}} peaked around the average value.

def: Function giving probability that a variable is below a value.
ex: The {{cumulative distribution function}} reached one at the maximum.

def: Long-run average outcome of a random variable weighted by probability.
ex: The {{expected value}} of the bet was slightly negative.

def: Most frequently occurring value in a dataset or distribution.
ex: The {{mode}} of the survey was the middle rating.

def: Difference between the largest and smallest values in data.
ex: The {{range}} of ages spanned from eighteen to sixty.

def: Rescaling data to zero mean and unit standard deviation.
ex: Applying {{standardization}} let features share a common comparable scale.

def: Cut point dividing a distribution into equal-probability intervals.
ex: The ninetieth {{quantile}} marked the highest-spending customers.

def: Probability that two or more events occur together.
ex: The {{joint probability}} of rain and delay was low.

def: Probability of an event given that another has occurred.
ex: The {{conditional probability}} of churn rose after complaints.

def: Probability of a single event ignoring other variables.
ex: The {{marginal probability}} of purchase summed across all channels.

def: Formula relating conditional probabilities to update beliefs with evidence.
ex: {{Bayes' theorem}} converted the prior into a posterior probability.

def: Variable whose value results from a random phenomenon.
ex: The dice roll is a {{random variable}} with six outcomes.

def: Events whose occurrence does not affect each other's probability.
ex: Two coin flips are {{independent events}} with unrelated outcomes.

def: Research measuring variables without assigning any deliberate intervention.
ex: The {{observational study}} tracked habits without changing behavior.

def: Distortion from a non-representative way of choosing subjects.
ex: Clear {{selection bias}} arose because only volunteers joined the study.

def: Error from analyzing only surviving cases and ignoring failures.
ex: Strong {{survivorship bias}} inflated the estimated success rate of startups.

def: Improvement caused by expectation rather than the actual treatment.
ex: The {{placebo effect}} explained some reported symptom relief.

def: Concealing group assignment to reduce bias in experiments.
ex: Double {{blinding}} prevented both patients and doctors knowing groups.

def: Extent to which findings apply beyond the studied sample.
ex: Limited diversity reduced the {{generalizability}} of the conclusions.

def: Degree to which a study establishes a genuine causal link.
ex: Randomization strengthened the {{internal validity}} of the trial.

def: Extent results generalize to other settings, people, and times.
ex: A narrow sample limited the {{external validity}} of findings.

def: Average value computed from the observations in a sample.
ex: The {{sample mean}} approximated the unknown population average.

def: Weighted average variance estimated across two or more groups.
ex: The t-test used {{pooled variance}} assuming equal group spreads.

def: Test comparing means of two related, matched measurements.
ex: A {{paired t-test}} compared scores before and after training.

def: Method combining regression and variance analysis controlling for covariates.
ex: An {{analysis of covariance}} adjusted outcomes for baseline differences.

def: Test making no assumptions about the data's underlying distribution.
ex: They chose a {{nonparametric test}} for the skewed ratings.

def: Nonparametric test comparing distributions of two independent groups.
ex: The {{Mann-Whitney U test}} compared the two unequal samples.

def: Nonparametric test comparing paired samples using ranked differences.
ex: The {{Wilcoxon signed-rank test}} handled the non-normal paired data.

def: Test comparing a sample against a reference cumulative distribution.
ex: The {{Kolmogorov-Smirnov test}} checked whether data were normal.

def: Expected proportion of false positives among rejected null hypotheses.
ex: Controlling the {{false discovery rate}} curbed spurious significant results.

def: Increased false positives arising from testing many hypotheses simultaneously.
ex: The {{multiple comparisons problem}} inflated the chance of errors.

def: Adjustment dividing the significance level by number of tests.
ex: A {{Bonferroni correction}} tightened thresholds across many comparisons.

def: Calculation determining the sample size needed to detect effects.
ex: A {{power analysis}} showed two hundred users were required.

def: Smallest effect an experiment can reliably identify as significant.
ex: The {{minimum detectable effect}} was set at two percent.

def: Mathematical representation of relationships among random variables in data.
ex: The {{statistical model}} captured how price affected demand.

def: Approach defining probability as long-run frequency of events.
ex: Classic {{frequentist statistics}} interpreted the p-value through repeated sampling.

def: Bayesian range containing a parameter with stated probability.
ex: The ninety-percent {{credible interval}} summarized posterior uncertainty.

def: Table displaying frequency counts across combinations of categorical variables.
ex: The {{contingency table}} cross-tabulated gender against purchase choice.

def: Ratio comparing the odds of an outcome between groups.
ex: An {{odds ratio}} above one indicated higher risk in smokers.

def: Ratio of outcome probability between exposed and unexposed groups.
ex: The {{relative risk}} doubled among the treated participants.

def: Recurring periodic fluctuations in data tied to calendar cycles.
ex: Sales showed clear {{seasonality}} peaking every December.

def: Correlation of a series with its own lagged values.
ex: Strong {{autocorrelation}} meant yesterday predicted today's traffic.

def: Property where a series' statistical characteristics stay constant over time.
ex: The model assumed {{stationarity}} of the differenced series.

def: Average of recent values recomputed as the window slides.
ex: A seven-day {{moving average}} smoothed the noisy daily counts.

def: Nonrandom sampling filling preset quotas for population subgroups.
ex: Using {{quota sampling}} ensured equal numbers from each age band.

def: Sampling entire naturally occurring groups rather than individuals.
ex: Efficient {{cluster sampling}} surveyed whole schools instead of scattered students.

def: Selecting elements at fixed regular intervals from an ordered list.
ex: Simple {{systematic sampling}} picked every tenth customer from the file.

def: Complete enumeration measuring every member of a population.
ex: A full {{census}} counted every household in the region.

def: Adjusting observations so the sample better reflects the population.
ex: Survey {{weighting}} corrected for the underrepresented younger group.
